author: boma_naps
content: Cross posting to get CL dev input on standardizing default JWT secret paths https://github.com/ethereum/execution-apis/pull/297  would simplify user setup by removing need for manual --jwt-secret configuration. Looking for client team positions: https://github.com/ethereum/pm/issues/1941#issuecomment-3984157106

author: tersec
content: I'd have to check, but I'm not sure if Nimbus still supported this, since it was kind of useless at the time with zero EL support for years. but it doesn't seem broadly unreasonable

author: tersec
content: it was (at the time) a non-standard which interop'd with no one and nothing

author: tersec
content: that's not an issue with the proposal though, that's an issue with the standardization-status of it

author: tersec
content: but. I'd distinguish between fixed jwt-secret _paths_ and fixed jwt secrets

author: tersec
content: the former gets quickly into OS-dependent stuff

author: tersec
content: the latter has tradeoffs but at least doesn't

author: boma_naps
content: Thanks for the context on this. Will check if other teams have a preference for the #302 approach instead.

author: nflaig
content:
as for Lodestar, we are fine with implementing a default path/behavior, currently we will not set `Authorization: Bearer ...` if the flag is omitted which isn't too useful unless ELs add a flag to disable auth

that said, this problem seems to be solved by tooling already and CL/EL a lot of the time don't share the same file system so I am nto sure how impactful this is

author: tersec
content: one consideration of default path is, e.g., it has to either be "windows-friendly" or ignore Windows. Yes, I know, ~no one stakes on Windows. But to randomly enshrine POSIX filesystem paths in the spec actually isn't something that exists now

author: tersec
content: even macOS paths look different in details/platform-expectations. And the file version needs nuance to even address cases of, e.g., multiple user accounts one for a CL, one for an EL, and a shared directory they can both read from (for JWT secret)

author: tersec
content: it's easy to accidentally prescribe a very specific setup schema

author: boma_naps
content: <@586161934425128960>  <@654267572107083777> thanks for the feedback. Should we then revisit #302 (fixed secret + CORS guidance) as a simpler alternative, or downgrade #297 to MAY/SHOULD?

author: tersec
content: it's simple and solves the problem described. if people have reverse-proxy stuff going on, that's on them and their setup shouldn't use this anyway

author: canercdm
content:
hi <@755590043632140352>! is there a feature planned for automated builder discovery? i'm with Flashbots and we've been thinking of the options around the builder configuration for CL clients - supposing this could get inflexible both for proposers and builders, and make builder entry harder, if proposers have to manually manage a list. we've been thinking this could be managed from a type of standardized smart contract interface which allows
- adding/removing builders, adjusting mgmt roles for this,
- getting the latest list of builders with their domain names like builder1.foobuilder.xyz.
could Prysm take a few configured contract addresses to load the latest builders from such builder registries? how would you feel about supporting this kind of feature discovery feature in Prysm? and are there any alternatives considered? curious what you think - thanks!

author: potuz
content:
I think the ultimate such approach would be to have a contract on chain that clients poll and take the list of endpoints for each builder. Next to that is a standard for some community maintained endpoints that provide lists of builders and clients poll regularly, like what checkpoint sync does now that clients understand that out of protocol mechanism. Next to that is just a community maintained list of builders that the node operator needs to check to add periodically to their configs. 

I think 1) is overcomplicated to even think about at this moment. 3) doesn't require any team work now but also I think quickly dies cause NO never change their settings, I myself may be signalling 30M gas limit on my node. I think we should be reallistically designing and pushing for 2 at the moment. This can be quickly implemented and checked by clients without having to deal with on-chain components.

author: canercdm
content: thanks for the quick response. indeed it makes sense that 2 would be the easiest and fastest as a short term discovery improvement. but so iiuc, if a smart contract standard (as in 1) stabilizes/matures in the longer term, you would find that more preferable over 2, to support from Prysm - is that correct?
